content: A bucket filled with water is revolved in a vertical circle of radius 10 m and water just not fall down. The time period of revolution will be (acceleration due to gravity $=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
explanation:
When a bucket full of water is rotated in a vertical circle, water will not spill only if velocity of bucket at the highest point is $\geq \sqrt{g r}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} v & =\sqrt{g r} \\ \therefore \quad & \omega=\frac{v}{r}=\sqrt{\frac{g}{r}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{~T}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{r}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{~T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{10}{10}} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{~T}=2 \pi \mathrm{~s} \end{array} $$

content: The angular separation between the minute hand and the hour hand of clock 40 minutes passed after $12 \mathrm{p} . \mathrm{m}$. is
explanation:
Degree moved by hour hand, for 1 revolution $=360^{\circ}$

for 1 hour $=\frac{360^{\circ}}{12}=30^{\circ}$

for $1 \mathrm{~min}=\frac{30}{60}=0.5^{\circ}$

$\therefore$ for $40 \mathrm{~min}=40 \times 0.5^{\circ}=20^{\circ}$

Degree moved by minute hand

for 1 hour $=360^{\circ}$

for $1 \mathrm{~min}=\frac{360}{60}=6^{\circ}$

$\therefore \quad$ for $40 \mathrm{~min}=6 \times 40^{\circ}=240^{\circ}$

Hence, at 12.40 pm

Angular separation $=240^{\circ}-20^{\circ}=220^{\circ}$

content: A string of length $L$ is fixed at one end and carries a mass m at the other end. The string makes $\frac{3}{\pi}$ revolutions per second around the vertical axis through the fixed end. The tension in the string is
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Given } \mathrm{n}=\frac{3}{\pi} \mathrm{rps} . \\ \therefore & \omega=2 \pi \mathrm{n}=6 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} . \\ & \text { Referring the figure, } \\ & T \sin \theta=m \omega^2 \mathrm{R}=m \omega^2(\mathrm{~L} \sin \theta) \\\therefore & T=m \omega^2 \mathrm{~L}=36 \mathrm{~mL} \end{array} $$

content: Which one of the following statements is false for a particle moving in a circle with constant angular speed?

options.content: linear velocity vector is along the tangent to the circle.
linear acceleration vector is along the tangent to the circle.
linear acceleration vector points to the centre of the circle.
the velocity and acceleration vectors are perpendicular to each other.

explanation:
Let us analyze each option one by one:

Option A:

Linear velocity vector is along the tangent to the circle.

  • This is true. In uniform circular motion, velocity is always tangent to the circle.

Option B:

Linear acceleration vector is along the tangent to the circle.

  • This is false. In uniform circular motion, acceleration is centripetal, that is, it points towards the centre of the circle and is not tangent.

Option C:

Linear acceleration vector points to the centre of the circle.

  • This is true. The centripetal acceleration always acts towards the centre.

Option D:

The velocity and acceleration vectors are perpendicular to each other.

  • This is true for uniform circular motion. Velocity is tangent and acceleration is radial (towards centre), so they are perpendicular.

Correct Answer:

Option B is false.

content: In case of well of death which is a vertical cylindrical wall of radius ' $r$ ' inside which vehicle is driven in horizontal circles. If ' $m$ ' is mass of vehicle, ' $V$ ' is the velocity and ' $\mu_s$ ' is the coefficient of static friction between the wheels of vehicle and walls then correct relation is [ $\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity]
explanation:
Consider the forces on the vehicle (mass $m$) pressed against the vertical wall as it moves in a circle of radius $r$ at speed $V$:

Normal force $N$ (horizontal) provides the centripetal force:

$$N = \frac{mV^2}{r}.$$

Static friction $f$ (vertical, upward) prevents the vehicle from sliding down. Its maximum value is

$$f_{\max} = \mu_s\,N = \mu_s\,\frac{mV^2}{r}.$$

For the vehicle not to slip, this friction must at least balance its weight $mg$:

$$\mu_s\,\frac{mV^2}{r}\;\ge\;mg.$$

Cancel $m$ and rearrange:

$$\mu_s\,\frac{V^2}{r}\;\ge\;g\quad\Longrightarrow\quad V^2 \;\ge\;\frac{rg}{\mu_s}.$$

Hence the correct relation is

$$V^2 \;\ge\;\frac{rg}{\mu_s},$$

which is option C.
